title: Roughing a spoon blank: axe to knife
subtitle: How to take a green-ash billet from log to knife-ready form
excerpt: The axe-to-knife rough-out is the phase of spoon-carving that most beginners skip or rush. This technique guide covers reading the grain in a green-ash billet before the first axe stroke, the sequence of axe cuts that take the blank from round log to flat face, and the point at which the axe work ends and the knife work begins.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for axe technique; the axe-to-knife blank-roughing sequence is documented in Victorian and Edwardian craft manuals.

content.text: The axe is faster than the knife and removes more wood per stroke; the knife is more precise. The rough-out phase establishes the spoon silhouette; everything from this point forward is refinement. A good rough-out reduces the knife work by 60 per cent. A bad rough-out; one that has come too close on one side or left too much on another; will dictate the finished spoon's dimensions whether the carver intends it or not.

Axe safety: both hands on the axe handle throughout. The cut ends off the end of the workpiece or into the chopping block; never into air near another body part. Keep the off-hand behind the axe line.
Sharpening: the carving axe must be sharp enough to shave hair off the forearm. Strop before starting and after every 20 minutes of use.
Workspace: a chopping block at knee height or a side-axe block. Never axe a blank held in the air; it must be supported on a firm surface.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach.
When to use this technique
Axe rough-out applies to any spoon project starting from a round
billet
or a large
riven

half-billet; eating spoon, cooking spoon, ladle, spatula. Spoons starting from a thin-walled branch that is already close to the finished thickness (under 4 cm diameter) can skip the axe entirely and start with the sloyd knife.
What you need
A carving axe (short-handled, curved bit), a side-axe block or a low chopping stump, a sloyd knife, and a leather strop. The billet is
green
ash: split in half so the flat face shows the grain.
Step-by-step

Read the grain on the flat face of the split billet. Ash grain usually runs nearly parallel to the billet axis; occasional diagonal lines indicate where branches joined. Draw the spoon profile in pencil on the flat face, keeping the handle axis aligned with the straightest grain section.

Place the billet flat-face-down on the chopping block. The curved face is uppermost; the task now is to reduce the curved back to roughly the finished back-of-spoon profile. Make axe cuts from the handle end toward the bowl end on the handle section, and from the bowl end toward the handle on the bowl section. Reading which direction goes with the grain requires a test cut: one stroke, observe whether it lifts a clean chip or dives into the wood. If it dives, reverse direction.

Flip the billet flat-face-up. The flat face is now the front of the spoon. Draw the side profile in pencil: the depth of the bowl depression relative to the handle plane. Remove waste from the sides of the billet, approaching but not reaching the pencil lines, with axe cuts made perpendicular to the long axis.
The
blank
is ready for the knife when: all major waste is removed, the handle profile is roughed out within 5 mm of the finished line, and the bowl area is roughly the correct oval shape. From here,
push cuts
on the sloyd knife refine the handle, neck, and bowl back; the hook knife then hollows the bowl.
What it looks like when it is right

A well-roughed spoon blank has a handle with recognisable rectangular cross-section (not round; that comes from the sloyd knife), a bowl oval that is still 8 to 10 mm oversize on each dimension, and no axe damage within 5 mm of the pencil profile line on any face. Axe marks are visible and expected; they will be removed in the knife phase. A rough-out that has cut into the handle profile on one side means the sloyd knife must correct an error rather than refine a form.
Common mistakes
Drawing the profile and then immediately trying to axe to the line: the axe is not a precision tool. Stay 5 to 8 mm from the pencil line; the knife crosses the last 5 mm.

Cutting all strokes in the same direction regardless of grain: the most common cause of a chip that dives into the billet and splits out a chunk of the intended spoon. Test one stroke, observe, then proceed.

Trying to produce a finished surface with the axe: the rough-out phase ends with a faceted surface; that is the correct result. Polish the rough-out and you have spent more time on it than the knife phase it was supposed to accelerate.
